docs.title: Knowledge Curator
docs.text: Automated weekly digest of high-signal content across AI systems, distributed systems, economics, complexity science, and philosophy.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
Every Sunday at 8am UTC, a GitHub Action:

  1. Fetches articles from configured RSS feeds published in the past 7 days
  2. Sends them to Claude (claude-sonnet-4-6) with a scoring prompt tuned for architectural durability
  3. Publishes the result here and commits it to vault/digests/ for Obsidian

docs.title: Digest Sections
docs.text: Section Signal Time 🔴 Read This Week High priority, clear architectural value < 30 min each 🟡 Weekend Deep Dive High depth, slower burn 45+ min 🔵 Cross-Domain Spark Outside primary domains, high leverage varies 🗄️ Archive Reference Worth keeping, not urgent varies

docs.title: Recent Digests
docs.text: See the Digests section in the nav for all weekly issues.

docs.title: Tuning
docs.text:
• Sources: edit sources.yaml — no code changes required
  • Scoring criteria: edit prompt_template.md — adjust domains, filtering rules, output format
  • Manual trigger: use workflow_dispatch on the GitHub Actions tab

docs.title: Reading List
docs.text: Manually curated backlog — items worth reading that aren't part of the weekly digest cycle. Organized by time commitment and domain.

docs.title: Local-First Software — Kleppmann et al., 2019
docs.text:
Domain: Distributed Systems / Knowledge Management | ~45 min

The foundational essay for offline-capable, user-owned data systems. Direct relevance to agent memory design: the seven ideals (no spinners, your data survives the cloud, works offline, collaboration without servers) map cleanly onto constraints for autonomous agent tooling. Read before designing any agent persistence layer.

docs.title: The Book of Why — Pearl, Chapters 1–3
docs.text:
Domain: Causal Inference / AI Foundations | ~60 min

Establishes the ladder of causation (association → intervention → counterfactuals). Changes how you reason about what LLMs can and cannot do — they're stuck on the first rung. Pairs with Pearl's 2018 paper below for the technical argument.

docs.title: Designing Data-Intensive Applications — Kleppmann
docs.text:
Domain: Distributed Systems | Ongoing

Focus: chapters on replication, consistency models, and CRDTs. Read through the lens of LangGraph agent state — where does eventual consistency cause problems in multi-agent workflows? Where are linearizability guarantees actually required?

docs.title: Antifragile — Taleb
docs.text:
Domain: Systems Thinking / Risk | Ongoing

Cross-domain: systems that gain from disorder. Maps directly to resilience architecture — what's the difference between robust (survives shocks) and antifragile (improves from them)? Applies to both software systems and investment portfolios. Chainlink oracle design as a case study.

docs.title: Chainlink Whitepaper + Oracle Security Threat Model
docs.text:
Domain: Distributed Systems / Crypto | ~2 hours total

  • Chainlink 2.0 Whitepaper
  • Oracle Manipulation: How it works

Feeds the investment thesis with systems-level intuition on cascade risk. Key question: under what failure modes does oracle consensus break, and how does the incentive design handle adversarial nodes?

docs.title: Symbol Grounding Problem — SEP
docs.text:
Domain: Philosophy of Mind / AI | ~20 min

Settles the debate framing on LLM intentionality. The Chinese Room argument and its rebuttals. Useful when distinguishing "understands" from "models the distribution of tokens that follow from understanding."

docs.title: Evolutionary Architecture and Fitness Functions — Fowler
docs.text:
Domain: Software Architecture | ~30 min

The bliki series on treating architectural properties as testable constraints. Fitness functions as architectural unit tests — maps to how agent evaluation should be structured.

docs.title: Theoretical Impediments to Machine Learning with Seven Sparks from the Causal Revolution — Pearl, 2018
docs.text:
Domain: AI / Causal Inference | ~30 min

The academic core of the causality argument. Seven things ML cannot do without causal structure. Pairs with The Book of Why chapters 1–3.

docs.title: Weekly Digests
docs.text:
All weekly knowledge digests, newest first.

  • June 14, 2026

docs.title: 🔴 Read This Week
docs.text: High signal, under 30 min each

docs.title: [AINews] Loopcraft: The Art of Stacking Loops
docs.text:
Source: Latent Space | Domain: Ai Systems | ~15 min

Why it matters: Karpathy, Steinberger, and Cherny's loop-stacking framing offers a composable architectural primitive for reasoning about agent control flow and feedback hierarchies that generalizes beyond any single framework.

docs.title: [AINews] Open Models, Model Labs vs Agent Labs, and What's Untrainable — Sarah Guo
docs.text:
Source: Latent Space | Domain: Ai Systems | ~20 min

Why it matters: The model-lab vs. agent-lab distinction and the concept of 'untrainable' capabilities is a durable architectural framing for understanding where value accretes in the AI stack and how to design systems around capability boundaries.

docs.title: The Nationalization of American Science
docs.text:
Source: Marginal Revolution | Domain: Economics | ~12 min

Why it matters: The proposed shift from state-funded but not state-directed science (the Vannevar Bush compact) to centrally directed grant governance is a structural mechanism-design inflection point with multi-decade consequences for research infrastructure.

docs.title: How Terry Tao Became an Evangelist for AI in Math
docs.text:
Source: Quanta Magazine | Domain: Ai Systems | ~18 min

Why it matters: Tao's decompose-verify-reassemble methodology using automated proof-checkers is a concrete architectural pattern for human-AI collaborative reasoning that transfers directly to formal verification in distributed systems and smart contract design.

docs.title: The no-human future
docs.text:
Source: Aeon Essays | Domain: Philosophy | ~25 min

Why it matters: Land's accelerationism provides the sharpest philosophical stress-test for AI alignment and techno-optimist assumptions, and understanding its internal logic is necessary for anyone designing systems at civilizational scale.

docs.title: 🟡 Weekend Deep Dive
docs.text: High depth, 45+ min

docs.title: The SPACE of AI
docs.text:
Source: ACM Queue | Domain: Distributed Systems | ~35 min

Why it matters: ACM Queue architectural frameworks have historically anchored how practitioners measure and reason about system performance; applying the SPACE productivity framework to AI systems could redefine evaluation criteria for human-AI collaborative workflows.

docs.title: Again, the research paper format will be dying out
docs.text:
Source: Marginal Revolution | Domain: Philosophy | ~20 min

Why it matters: 'The Last Human-Written Paper' argument is a structural claim about knowledge representation formats and institutional epistemology that will reshape how AI-generated knowledge is archived, verified, and attributed across every domain.

docs.title: 🔵 Cross-Domain Spark
docs.text: One item outside your primary domains

docs.title: How did Stanislaw Lem imagine advanced computer intelligence?
docs.text:
Source: Marginal Revolution | Domain: Philosophy | ~15 min

Why it matters: Lem's GOLEM—unpredictable, context-sensitive, possessed of alien humor—is a 40-year-old behavioral specification that maps with unsettling precision onto emergent LLM failure modes, bridging literary theory, alignment research, and complexity science in a single frame.

docs.title: 🗄️ Archive Reference
docs.text: Noteworthy but not urgent

docs.title: What shape is the Earth?
docs.text:
Source: Aeon Essays | Domain: Science | ~20 min

Why it matters: The epistemological lesson that precision measurement drives paradigm shifts—not theory alone—is a durable cross-domain principle applicable to benchmark design, oracle data quality, and the philosophy of scientific infrastructure.
